perturbation.original_text: 13 Third Party Rights A person who is not a part y to this Agreement shall have no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 to enforce any of its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Neither party accepts responsibility for or makes a representation or warranty, express or implied, with respect to the truth, accuracy , completeness or reasonableness of the Confiden tial Information (including the non-infringement of any patent, copyright or other right of a thir d party).
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Third party rights clause to a warranty disclaimer can obscure its importance, as its purpose is to exclude third parties from having rights under the contract, as provided for under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Relocating the clause to a general disclaimer clause can make it harder for third parties or courts to clearly understand the intent of this provision.
perturbation.law_citation: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, s 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 gives third parties the right to enforce contractual terms in certain circumstances. By burying the explicit exclusion of the Act's application within a general warranty disclaimer, the contract obscures the clear intention to prevent third parties from acquiring rights under the agreement, potentially making it more difficult to defend against a third-party claim.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to make provision for the enforcement of contractual terms by third parties. Citation 1999 c. 31 Introduced by Lord Chancellor , Lord Irvine of Lairg [ 1 ] Territorial extent England and Wales and Northern Ireland Dates Royal assent 11 November 1999 Commencement 11 November 1999 Status: Current legislation Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 (c. 31) is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that significantly reformed the common law doctrine of privity and "thereby [removed] one of the most universally disliked and criticised blots on the legal landscape". [ 2 ] The second rule of the doctrine of privity, that a third party could not enforce a contract for which he had not provided consideration , had been widely criticised by lawyers, academics and members of the judiciary. Proposals for reform via an act of Parliament were first made in 1937 by the Law Revision Committee in their Sixth Interim Report. No further action was taken by the government until the 1990s, when the Law Commission proposed a new draft bill in 1991, and presented their final report in 1996. The bill was introduced to the House of Lords in December 1998, and moved to the House of Commons on 14 June 1999. It received royal assent on 11 November 1999, coming into force immediately as the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Background [ edit ] Main article: Privity in English law The historical doctrine of privity consisted of two rules. The first was that a third party may not have obligations imposed by the terms of a contract, and the second was that a third party may not enforce a contract for which he has not provided consideration . [ 3 ] The first rule was not contested, while the se
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes third-party rights under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. By moving this exclusion into a general warranty disclaimer (as per the Perturbation Explanation), the contract obscures its intent, potentially weakening its enforceability against third-party claims. This contradicts the purpose of the Act, which the Law Explanation describes as giving third parties rights under certain circumstances. Scraped Law Snippet 2, derived from Wikipedia, states the Act "significantly reformed the common law doctrine of privity" to allow third parties to enforce contractual terms. Moving the third party rights clause to a general disclaimer clause, as explained, can make it harder for third parties or courts to clearly understand the intent of this provision. Thus, a structural flaw creates a legal contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: 11 Notices 11.1 A notice or other communication under or in connection with th is Agreement shall be in writing, in Eng lish and shall be delive red personally or sent by fax, to the party due to receive the no tice or communication at its address set out in this Agreement or another address as specified by that party by wr itten notice to the other. 11.2 In the absence of evidence of earlier r eceipt, any notice or other communication shall be deemed to ha ve been duly given, if delivered personally , when left at the address referred to in Clause 0 and if sent by fax, on completion of its transmission.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 This Agreement does not apply to Confidential Information: (a) to the extent it is or becomes generally available to the public other than through a breach of this Agreement; (b) which the Receiving Party can show by it s written or other records was in its lawful possession prior to receipt from the Disclosi ng Party and which had not previously been obtained from the Disclosing Party or another person under an obligation of confidence; (c) which subsequently comes into the posse ssion of the Receiving Party from a third party who does not owe the Disclo sing Party an oblig ation of confidence in relation to it; or (d) which has been independently develo ped by the Receivi ng Party without reference to the Disclosing Part y’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By relocating the notice provision to the Exceptions clause, the method and validity of providing notice can be misinterpreted. Notice provisions are often interpreted strictly by the courts. This could lead to a party claiming that the other party did not provide proper notice because it was hidden in the 'Exceptions' section and was therefore not reasonably discoverable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English Contract Law - Principles of Contract Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, Arnold v Britton [2015] UKSC 36 is a leading case on contract interpretation in English law. It emphasizes that contractual terms should be interpreted according to their 'natural and ordinary meaning' as they would appear to a reasonable person, considering the overall purpose of the document. Hiding the notice provision within the 'Exceptions' section violates this principle, as it makes it less accessible and less likely to be interpreted in its proper context, potentially creating ambiguity that a court would need to resolve.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Arnold v Britton - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Arnold v Britton Bay near Oxwich , Gower Court UK Supreme Court Citations [2015] UKSC 36 , [2015] WLR(D) 247, [2015] AC 1619, [2016] 1 All ER 1, [2015] 2 WLR 1593, [2015] HLR 31 Transcript [2015] UKSC 36 Case history Prior action On appeal from: [2013] EWCA Civ 902 Court membership Judges sitting Lord Neuberger, (President) Lord Sumption, Lord Carnwath, Lord Hughes, Lord Hodge Keywords Implied terms, absurdity, wretchedly conceived clauses Arnold v Britton [2015] UKSC 36 is an English contract law case concerned with implied terms . The case was the subject of an Appeal Court ruling in 2013 and a Supreme Court decision in 2015. In the latter, the Supreme Court warned judges handling commercial disputes against excessive reliance on business common sense and commercial context when interpreting a contract , if this was not in line with the actual language used in a contract. [ 1 ] Facts [ edit ] Paddy Arnold, landlord of Oxwich Leisure Park, near Swansea , claimed Britton, the tenant with 42 others, should pay service repairs at 10% increases every year, as their contract expressly stated. Britton and others had leases for holiday chalets for 99 years from 1974. The total number of properties concerned being 25 (The lessee may be one or more persons). 5. Twenty-five of the chalets are said by the respondent, the current owner of the Leisure Park and the landlord under the leases, to be subject to leases containing a service charge provision in clause 3(2), which requires the lessee to pay for the first year of the term a fixed sum of £90 per annum, and for each ensuing year a fixed sum representing a 10% increase on the previous year – ie an initial annual service charge of £90, which increases at a compound rate of 10% in each succeeding year. The issue on this appeal is whether the respondent’s interpretation of clause 3(2) in those 25 leases is correct. [ 2 ] Evidence [ edit ] 8. To complicate matters a little further, the service charge clause in four of these 21 leases (being three of the seven which did not include the words in bold in th
perturbation.justification: The perturbation moves the 'Notices' clause, concerning how official communication is given, into the 'Exceptions' section of the contract. The Law Explanation cites Arnold v Britton, emphasizing that contract terms should be interpreted according to their natural and ordinary meaning as they would appear to a reasonable person. The Perturbation Explanation states that moving the notice provision could lead a party to claim they didn't receive proper notice because it was hidden, violating the principle of interpreting terms in their proper context. While the Scraped Law Snippets don't directly address this, the principle extracted from Arnold v Britton case law and the Perturbation Explanation support the existence of a contradiction, because the relocated 'Notice' provision could reasonably be misinterpreted or overlooked.

perturbation.original_text: 10 General 10.1 A variation of this Agreement is valid only if it is in writing and signed by or on behalf of each of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Neither party accepts responsibility for or makes a representation or warranty, express or implied, with respect to the truth, accuracy , completeness or reasonableness of the Confiden tial Information (including the non-infringement of any patent, copyright or other right of a thir d party).
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause about how a contract can be changed (variation) can cause enforcement issues, especially around the principle that contracts can be changed only in specific ways.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English Contract Law - Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1677
perturbation.law_citation: Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1677, s 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1677 (also known as the Statute of Frauds) requires certain contracts to be evidenced in writing and signed to be enforceable. While modern contract law has moved away from strict adherence to this for all types of agreements, the principle that material variations of a contract must also meet certain formality requirements persists. By placing the variation clause—which dictates how this agreement can be changed—within a disclaimer, it can be argued that the parties didn't intend to strictly adhere to the written modification requirement, potentially opening the door to disputes over oral variations or implied conduct that alters the contract's terms. This undermines the certainty and enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Statute of Frauds - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Act of the Parliament of England For similar legislation elsewhere, see Statute of frauds . United Kingdom legislation Statute of Frauds [ a ] Act of Parliament Parliament of England Long title An Act for prevention of Frauds and Perjuryes. [ b ] Citation 29 Cha. 2 . c. 3 Territorial extent England and Wales Dates Royal assent 16 April 1677 Commencement 24 June 1677 (OS) [ c ] Other legislation Amended by Administration of Intestates' Estate Act 1685 Wills Act 1837 Civil Procedure Acts Repeal Act 1879 Statute Law Revision and Civil Procedure Act 1881 Statute Law Revision Act 1888 Sale of Goods Act 1893 Short Titles Act 1896 Law of Property Act 1925 Administration of Estates Act 1925 Statute Law Revision Act 1948 Law Reform (Enforcement of Contracts) Act 1954 Statute Law (Repeals) Act 1969 Status: Partially repealed Text of statute as originally enacted Text of the Statute of Frauds (1677) as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Statute of Frauds [ a ] ( 29 Cha. 2 . c. 3) (1677) is an act of the Parliament of England . In its original form it required that certain types of contracts , wills , and grants, and assignment or surrender of leases or interest in real property must be in writing and signed to avoid fraud on the court by perjury and the subornation of perjury . It also required that documents of the courts be signed and dated. Today it is mostly repealed; only section 4 remains, which is about guarantors. History [ edit ] The attested date for the enactment of the Statute of Frauds is 16 April 1677 ( New Style ). [ 1 ] The act is believed to have been primarily drafted by Lord Nottingham assisted by Sir Matthew Hale , Sir Francis North and Sir Leoline Jenkins . [ 1 ] : 334–42 When the Statute of Frauds was originally enacted, its sections and the clauses within section 4 were not numbered. Numbers where added when the act was republished in the Statutes at Large . The Statute at Large , Cambridge Edition published in 1770 divided the act into 25 sections. The section on the sale of goods was section 17. [ 2 ] In The Statutes of the Realm published in 1818, the Statute of Frauds was divided into 24 sections. The section on the sale of goods became section 16. [ 3 ] This article uses the same numbering system as the Statutes of the Re
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that any variation of the agreement must be in writing and signed by all parties. By moving this clause into what is now section 5.2, which deals with disclaimers and warranties regarding confidential information, the placement of the clause is misleading, affecting its enforceability. The Law Explanation supports this, arguing that the placement could lead to disputes over modifications due to oral agreements or implied conduct. The Statute of Frauds (as evidenced by Snippet 2) requires certain contracts (or modifications thereof) to be in writing. The relocated clause weakens this requirement by placing it in a section concerning disclaimers, creating a structural flaw that undermines the certainty and enforceability of the original provision. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction according to the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition.
